claim_text: Hyperinflation requires fiscal dominance AND collapsed real supply / external-FX obligation — not monetary expansion alone.
llm_reason: Generality gap: claim adds real-supply-collapse and FX-obligation conjuncts; hypothesis tests only fiscal-dominance as hyperinflation precondition.
hyp_claim: Every documented hyperinflation episode since 1900 (Weimar Germany, post-WW2 Hungary, Yugoslavia 1990s, Zimbabwe 2000s, Venezuela 2010s-2020s, among others) was preceded by fiscal dominance — a state of affairs where monetary policy is subordinated to financing government deficits that cannot be financed by taxation or market-rate borrowing. Monetary mismanagement alone, absent fiscal dominance, has not produced hyperinflation in the historical record.

claim_text: Zimbabwe 2000-2008 hyperinflation followed the real-supply collapse from land reform and external sanctions; currency expansion was downstream of real-capacity destruction, not causally independent.
llm_reason: Hypothesis tests Zimbabwe 2000-2009 FTLRP and RBZ monetisation producing collapse metrics, aligning with the claim's causal ordering.
hyp_claim: Zimbabwe's Fast Track Land Reform Programme (FTLRP, 2000-2002) combined with Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe deficit monetisation produced a canonical institutional and economic collapse 2000-2009 that manifests as >=7 of 10 pre-registered extreme-outcome metrics, each drawn from an independent data source and measuring a different causal layer (agricultural-capacity destruction, monetary collapse, output contraction, human-capital flight, humanitarian stress). The canonical-case claim is that no non-war peacetime sub-Saharan African economy in the 2000-2009 window matches even 4 of these 10 thresholds simultaneously; Zimbabwe matches most. A refutation (<=3 metrics met) would indicate that Zimbabwe's collapse has been substantially overstated or that the 1980-1999 pre-reform trend was already trending toward collapse independent of FTLRP and RBZ monetisation.

hyp_claim: US WWII fiscal expansion 1941-1945 (debt reaching 106% of GDP) did not produce sustained inflation once price controls were lifted and real capacity returned, consistent with MMT's real-constraint view.
